items.description: Airship No. 456 collapsed against mooring structure; severe failure of the lower frame.
Fire during landing phase; flames concentrated near the engine area, comparable to case 4.
Envelope collapsed beneath mooring tower; deformation consistent with failed docking procedure.
Combustion spreading along the lower envelope; probable origin near the engine group, see case 2.
Wreckage recovered near the shoreline; forward section submerged and torn by impact.
Airship broken in coastal waters; clear frame separation along the central section.
Deflated envelope in urban canal; structure compromised despite largely intact outer covering.
Airship burning after ground impact; advanced combustion of the rear envelope.
Hull grounded on sloped terrain; wide side tear and visible impact damage.
Fire developing beneath the envelope during ground operation; initial spread concentrated along the lower hull.
Rear structure exposed by fire; internal frame visible and envelope still partially intact.
Envelope engulfed by flames on landing field; more advanced development of case 11.
Extended fire during night operation; progressive collapse of the upper structure.
Detached tail section smoking on the water surface; fracture line suggests structural separation after impact.
Envelope engulfed by flames on landing field; more advanced development of case 13.
Hull descending in flames over open water; smoke column rising from the rear section.
Ground wreckage under inspection; torn side panels and exposed internal frame.
Airship grounded in mountainous terrain; forward envelope torn, with visible damage along the side.
Large rigid airship positioned near a mooring mast, tilted upward during landing or docking operations.
Airship burning near the ground with heavy smoke rising from the envelope during a catastrophic landing accident.
Airship after forced descent, smoke concentrated at the rear section while the forward hull remains suspended above ground.
Space II in final descent; fire begins along the lower stern and spreads upward, as in other cases of engine-side ignition.
Russian airship shown during a catastrophic descent, with flames and dense smoke rising from the rear section while the hull remains close to the ground.
Airship in steep final descent, with flames and dense smoke spreading along the side of the hull above an open field and distant buildings.
The wreckage of an airship following structural collapse; the front section of the gondola is still visible, while the metal frame appears twisted and collapsed along its entire upper section.
Smoke emerging from the lower forward section; probable engine-area ignition during field operation.
Airship rising at abnormal angle with smoke from lower hull; incident comparable to case 20.
Mountain wreckage after impact; outer envelope lost, with the internal frame exposed along the slope.
Night fire after structural collapse; frame remains visible while the envelope is almost entirely consumed.
Close view of the burned framework; combustion appears to have progressed through the lower structure first.
Wreckage burning on inclined terrain; impact and fire damage concentrated along the lower side.
Tail frame recovered after collapse; stabilizers distorted and rear structure heavily compressed.
Rear section under inspection; circular frame and stabilizers preserved despite severe central collapse.
Illuminated framework after fire; outer covering destroyed, leaving the internal structure fully exposed.
Ground wreckage after fire; envelope covering destroyed and smoke still rising from the rear section.
Night view of the hull burning in a mountain basin; fire spread along both lower edges of the frame.
Wreckage resting in wooded terrain; internal structure exposed, with no remaining envelope covering.
Forward frame recovered among trees; deformation suggests final impact followed by structural compression.
Close inspection of the passenger section; cabin remains partially intact within a collapsed frame.
Burned wreckage after impact; forward frame upright, with smoke rising from the central debris field.
Collapsed frame near mooring structure; tail and forward sections remain identifiable despite central failure.
Mountain wreckage with residual smoke; frame broken across the central hull after ground impact.
Open field inspection; long frame section collapsed but preserved enough for structural comparison.
Hillside wreckage still burning; forward frame intact while rear section is consumed by fire.
Night fire inside exposed frame; combustion concentrated along the lower structure and internal supports.
Scale model of the airship Aurelia II under inspection; suspension lines and gondola arrangement tested beneath artificial light.
Technical review of the Vistula Expedition model; officers comparing hull balance, tail plane position, and projected lift distribution.
Laboratory observation of the Severny-4 prototype model; crew studying cable tension, gondola load, and envelope stability during suspended test.
Classroom demonstration of the Columbia-B test model; instructor indicating forward pressure points and gondola suspension layout.
Technical review of the Iberia-7 model; observers studying gondola placement, rigging tension, and lower-frame balance.
Suspended test of the Severny-4 model; team examining cable load, envelope trim, and gondola stability under artificial light.
Early balance test for the Volga Research Flight model; temporary rigging used to measure tilt response and suspended load.
Inspection of the Doppelkarper Mark-Nine concept; twin-envelope arrangement examined for lift distribution and structural redundancy.
Public demonstration of the Adler-Klein model; scale hull used to explain rudder response and simplified control surfaces.
Demonstration of the Castilla-A model; exposed suspension lines show proposed gondola connection and ballast control method.
Wind-table preparation for the Rhein Versuch model; small-scale hull positioned before airflow testing and stability observation.
Wall-mounted plans and full model of the Cardoba cargo design; side elevation compared with gondola and ballast arrangement.
Review of the Krakow-P2 training model; committee notes focus on tail-plane proportion and cable suspension beneath the envelope.
Wind-tunnel test of the Atlantic Ranger scale hull; airflow trial arranged to measure drag along the ribbed envelope.
Presentation of the Aurelia II model; rigid gondola frame and lower support truss inspected before comparative stability tests.
Final adjustment of the Adriatico-6 scale model; operator checking tail alignment and underside fittings before documentation.
Kurier Warszawski report on the Polonia II disaster near Poznan; witness accounts describe fire, crash impact, and the still-incomplete list of victims.
The Guardian coverage of the Manchester Air-Dock catastrophe; investigators suspect static discharge and possible engine-area ignition.
Diario del Aire report on the Río de la Plata mail airship; electrical storm and suspected engine failure caused in-flight fire over the estuary.
ABC front page on the Cadiz aerial wreck; La Infanta caught fire before falling into the sea, with investigators examining possible gas or engine faults.
Gazeta Wyborcza account of the Warsaw-Praga crash; giant airship wreckage shown after impact, with early reports questioning weather and ignition.
Izvestia report on the Krasnaya Zarya accident over the Urals; rescue work continued while officials examined weather conditions and possible technical failure.
Russian business press report on the Slava Truda explosion near Leningrad; market panic followed the crash and renewed doubts over hydrogen airship safety.
Sibirskiy Vestnik report on the Arktika expedition; the airship is shown at a severe angle during adverse conditions, with no casualties reported.
Daily Mirror report on the Zephyr disaster over the Atlantic; survivors brought ashore at Halifax as inquiry records panic, fire, and failed control.
Pravda report on the Krasny Proletariy test flight; the Soviet airship caught fire near Moscow after a suspected rear gas-cell or engine failure.
Bild special on the Hindenburg II fire over Berlin; flames broke out during landing at Tempelhof, with witnesses reporting explosions before collapse.
La Vanguardia report on the Mediterraneo disaster over Barcelona; engine explosion and fire scattered wreckage across the city after the Marseille route crossing.
El Pais report on the Icaro crash in the Pyrenees; violent wind destroyed the hull during forced landing.
The Times report on the HMS Majestic collapse over the Thames; witnesses heard explosions before the passenger liner fell into the river in flames.
New York Post front page on the Liberty Island disaster; passenger liner exploded in mid-air over the harbor, with rescue boats recovering wreckage from the water.

Gazzetta del Mattino extraordinary edition on the Italia N.7 disaster at Superga; the airship caught fire during a demonstration manoeuvre near Turin, crashed into the hillside, and left only four survivors.

Berliner Tageblatt und Handels-Zeitung front page reporting a chronicle of dirigible disasters, with a large image of the LZ 129 Hindenburg in flames and dense German columns discussing fires, storms, technical failures, safety concerns, and the uncertain future of airship travel.

La Stampa front page reporting a fictional dirigible disaster involving the German airship LZ-90, shown in a large photograph burning over the North Sea. The page features dense Italian columns about the crash, rescue attempts, related dirigible incidents in France and Russia, aviation safety concerns, technical failures, and government responses to airship travel risks.
Annotated technical sheet for the Akron class, with full side elevations, internal compartment markings, gondola placement, and handwritten notes on risk, balance, and structural distribution.
Early sectional study for the Krasnaya Zarya, showing circular frame geometry, front views, and lower gondola arrangements before the final hull proportions were fixed.
Detailed construction drawing of the Krasnaya Zarya, combining side elevation, bow and stern sections, gondola supports, and internal frame studies used for the first Russian risk evaluations.
Structural plan for the Icaro, focused on the forward hull, passenger compartment, engine fittings, and lower suspension points later examined after the Pyrenees incident.
Italian design sheet related to the Italia polar programme, showing side elevations and frontal framing of the envelope, with notes on gondola weight and the vulnerability of the lower structure.
Preliminary technical plate for the Nobile expedition airship, including side views, circular frame sections, tail configuration, and handwritten calculations on lift and cabin suspension.
Comparative drawing of the Nobile airship, with multiple hull elevations, frame sections, and gondola placement studies; Adler marked it as useful for understanding polar navigation stress.
Early design proposal for the Polonia II, showing a long passenger hull, lower gondola, tail surfaces, and several cross-section studies prepared before the later Poznan service trials.
Revised Polish technical plan for the Polonia II after the Poznan catastrophe, with emphasis on gondola position, engine housings, and the lower hull areas suspected of structural weakness.
Conceptual design of the Hindenburg II program, featuring an aerodynamic rigid hull, tail surfaces, lower gondola modules, and notes on passenger seating arrangements.

Late technical reconstruction of the Hindenburg II, with side views, frame sections, and handwritten annotations on ignition points, outer skin failure, and the relation between gas cells and the lower service deck.
American design comparison for the Akron class, showing three hull profiles, reinforced lower gondolas, and notes on engine placement later compared with other Atlantic passenger-airship failures.
Technical sheet for the Liberty Island passenger liner, showing upper and lower side elevations with gondola and engine positions, preserved after the New York explosion investigation.
Spanish design sheet for the La Infanta, with paired side elevations, tail assembly, cabin placement, and handwritten inspection notes later attached to the Cadiz maritime accident file.

Simplified structural drawing of the Polonia II, combining a full side profile with three frontal frame sections; likely used by Adler to compare the original design with the wreckage recovered near Poznan.
German airship, 1932; envelope standing nearly vertical after loss of lift, with lower hull torn open near the ground and tail frame still visible above.
I note the Aurelia II at the edge of collapse; smoke rises from the lower hull while the envelope remains partially inflated, suggesting fire began before full ground contact.
Airship during uncontrolled descent; nose remains elevated while the damaged lower structure points toward the landing field, indicating rapid loss of buoyancy.
Italian airship Nobile after impact near open water; fire concentrated below the forward hull, with the envelope still rising from the surface as combustion spreads upward.
Airship Atlantic Ranger lifting at a dangerous angle after ignition near the rear engine bay; smoke remains dense at ground level while the forward envelope is still intact.
Italian airship Roma in near-vertical attitude after structural failure; smoke issues from the lower hull, with ground crew approaching despite continuing instability.

aboutItems.description: The former Anti-Aircraft Artillery office is reorganized after World War I, beginning its transformation into a department for aerial risk analysis.
Friedrich Adler studies the first airship incident files assigned to him in 1926, preparing to document the risks of transoceanic navigation.
Officers and engineers work together inside the new AAA research section, comparing maps, photographs, and technical reports on airship incidents.
Maps, instruments, and route charts are used to study the dangers of long-distance airship travel across the ocean.
Photographs, reports, and recovered fragments are examined as part of Adler’s method for understanding technical failures.
Photographs, notes, and case files are organized into a growing archive dedicated to the study and memory of airship accidents.
The AAA team compares different airship incidents, connecting images, maps, and technical observations to identify recurring risks.
The Adler Archive is moved to a new institution, where its files, boxes, and technical records are formally catalogued and preserved.
Adler and his colleagues examine meteorological patterns, treating weather as one of the key dangers in airship navigation.
Recognized as the first photograph showing Friedrich Adler at the scene of an airship accident.
